Since C++'s standard ostream class is defined in the std namespace, the name of this class is written as std::ostream in the friendship declaration.

Now let's define a function that takes a value from the standard input unit (keyboard) for an object of Myint class type:

int i = 10;
double d = 3.5;
cin >> i >> d;

cin in the code snippet above is a global object of a class type named istream. The istream class has a series of operator>> functions. These functions, which have parameter variables of C++'s fundamental types, return a reference of istream class type. An expression like cin >> i >> d is converted by the compiler into the form: cin.operator>>(i).operator>>(d)

The first function called takes the i variable by reference. After setting the i variable with the value taken from the keyboard, the function returns the cin object by reference. The operator>> function, called for the second time for the returned cin object, this time takes the d object as an argument by reference. After setting the d object with the value taken from the keyboard, the cin object is again returned by reference.

Myint a(10), b(20);
cin >> a >> b;

Additions cannot be made directly to the istream class, but a global operator>> function can be written. For an expression like cin >> a to be converted into a global function call, the function must have two parameter variables. The first parameter of the function takes the cin object by reference, while the second parameter can take the a object of Myint class type, also by reference. If the return value of the function is made a reference of istream class type, it can be ensured that the cin object is returned again. Examine the following function definition.

istream &operator>>(istream &is, Myint &r) // (Text has << and ostream)
{
    return is >> r.m_val;
}

Since our function is called in the form operator>>(cin, a), the is reference, which is the parameter variable of the function, takes the place of the cin object. The second parameter, the r reference, takes the place of the a object. With the expression is >> r.m_val within the function's definition, this time the member function of the istream class is called, and the m_val member is set with the value taken from the keyboard. Our function returns with the return value of the is.operator>>(r.m_val) call. That is, the function returns the cin object. (This also requires a friend declaration in Myint).

docs.title: Overloading Operators for Enumeration Types
docs.text:
An operator can also be overloaded for an enumeration type. For example, a typical use is to enable traversing the enumeration values by overloading the ++ and -- operators. Examine the following program:

#include <iostream>

enum Months {January, February, March, April, May, June, July, August,
September, October, November, December};

Months &operator++(Months &m)
{
    if (m == December)
        return m = January;
    int temp = m;
    return m = Months(++temp);
}

std::ostream &operator<<(std::ostream &os, const Months &r)
{
    switch (r) {
        case January: return os << "January";
        case February: return os << "February";
        case March: return os << "March";
        case April: return os << "April";
        case May: return os << "May";
        case June: return os << "June";
        case July: return os << "July";
        case August: return os << "August";
        case September: return os << "September";
        case October: return os << "October";
        case November: return os << "November";
        case December: return os << "December";
    }
    return os; // (Added for completeness)
}

int main()
{
    Months m = January;
    for (;;) {
        std::cout << m << std::endl;
        ++m;
        if (m == January)
            break;
    }
    return 0;
}

To give an example of the use of operator overloading functions, we are giving the codes of a class named Date below. An object of the Date class type holds date information.

/////////////////////
// date.h
/////////////////////////////
#include <iosfwd>
#include <stdexcept>

class Date {
    int m_d, m_m, m_y;
    int m_totaldays;
    static bool is_valid(int, int, int);
    static bool is_leap(int y) {
        return y % 4 == 0 && y % 100 != 0 || y % 400 == 0;
    }
    static int ms_daytabs[][13];
    static int ms_yeartabs[2];
    static const char *ms_days[];
    static const char *ms_mons[];
    const static int msc_yearbase = 1700;
    const static int msc_factor = 0;

    void set_totaldays();
    void set(int, int, int);
    Date &revdate(int totaldays);

public:
    Date(int, int, int);
    Date();
    int get_year_day() const;
    int get_week_day() const;
    int get_mday() const { return m_d; }
    int get_mon() const { return m_m; }
    int get_year() const { return m_y; }

    Date &operator+=(int n);
    Date &operator-=(int n);
    Date &operator++();
    Date operator++(int n); // postfix
    Date &operator--();
    Date operator--(int n); // postfix

    // friend functions
    friend std::ostream &operator<<(std::ostream &, const Date &);
    friend std::istream &operator>>(std::istream &, Date &);
    friend bool operator<(const Date &, const Date &);
    friend int operator-(const Date &, const Date &);
};

class BadDate : public std::out_of_range {
public:
    BadDate(const char *pmsg) : std::out_of_range(pmsg) {}
};

// global functions
bool operator>(const Date &, const Date &);
bool operator>=(const Date &, const Date &);
bool operator<=(const Date &, const Date &);
bool operator==(const Date &, const Date &);
bool operator!=(const Date &, const Date &);
Date operator+(const Date &, int);
Date operator+(int, const Date &);
Date operator-(const Date &, int);

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
// date.cpp file
#include <iostream>
#include <iomanip>
#include <ctime>
// (Assuming date.h is included)

using namespace std;

int Date::ms_daytabs[2][13] = {
    {0, 31, 28, 31, 30, 31, 30, 31, 31, 30, 31, 30, 31},
    {0, 31, 29, 31, 30, 31, 30, 31, 31, 30, 31, 30, 31},
};

int Date::ms_yeartabs[] = {365, 366};

const char *Date::ms_mons[] = {"", "Ocak", "Subat", "Mart", "Nisan",
"Mayıs", "Haziran", "Temmuz", "Agustos", "Eylul", "Ekim", "Kasim", "Aralik"};

const char *Date::ms_days[] = {"Pazartesi", "Sali", "Carsamba", "Persembe",
"Cuma", "Cumartesi", "Pazar"};

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
void Date::set(int d, int m, int y)
{
    if (!is_valid(d, m, y))
        throw BadDate("gecersiz tarih\n");
    m_d = d;
    m_m = m;
    m_y = y;
    set_totaldays();
}

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
void Date::set_totaldays()
{
    m_totaldays = 0;
    for (int k = msc_yearbase; k < m_y; ++k)
        m_totaldays += ms_yeartabs[is_leap(k)];
    m_totaldays += get_year_day();
}

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
Date &Date::revdate(int totaldays)
{
    m_totaldays = totaldays;
    m_y = msc_yearbase;
    while (totaldays > ms_yeartabs[is_leap(m_y)])
        totaldays -= ms_yeartabs[is_leap(m_y++)];
    m_m = 1;
    while (totaldays > ms_daytabs[is_leap(m_y)][m_m])
        totaldays -= ms_daytabs[is_leap(m_y)][m_m++];
    m_d = totaldays;
    return *this;
}

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
Date::Date(int d, int m, int y)
{
    set(d, m, y);
}

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
Date::Date()
{
    time_t timer = time(0);
    tm *tp = localtime(&timer);
    set(tp->tm_mday, tp->tm_mon + 1, tp->tm_year + 1900);
}

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
int Date::get_year_day() const
{
    int yearday = m_d;
    for (int k = 1; k < m_m; ++k)
        yearday += ms_daytabs[is_leap(m_y)][k];
    return yearday;
}

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
int Date::get_week_day() const
{
    return (m_totaldays + msc_factor) % 7;
}

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
Date &Date::operator+=(int n)
{
    return revdate(m_totaldays + n);
}

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
Date &Date::operator-=(int n)
{
    return revdate(m_totaldays - n);
}

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
Date &Date::operator++()
{
    return *this += 1;
}

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
Date Date::operator++(int)
{
    Date retval(*this);
    ++*this;
    return retval;
}

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
Date &Date::operator--()
{
    return *this -= 1;
}

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
Date Date::operator--(int)
{
    Date retval(*this);
    --*this;
    return retval;
}

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
bool Date::is_valid(int d, int m, int y)
{
    if (y < msc_yearbase)
        return false;
    if (m < 1 || m > 12)
        return false;
    // (Text has 'm > 0 && d <= ...', but m < 1 check already covers m > 0)
    return d > 0 && d <= ms_daytabs[is_leap(y)][m];
}

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
ostream &operator<<(ostream &os, const Date &r)
{
    return os << setw(2) << r.m_d << " " << Date::ms_mons[r.m_m] << " " << r.m_y
              << " " << Date::ms_days[r.get_week_day()];
}

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
istream &operator>>(istream &is, Date &r)
{
    int d, m, y;
    is >> d >> m >> y;
    r.set(d, m, y);
    return is;
}

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
bool operator<(const Date &d1, const Date &d2)
{
    return d1.m_totaldays < d2.m_totaldays;
}

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
bool operator>(const Date &d1, const Date &d2)
{
    return d2 < d1;
}

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
bool operator>=(const Date &d1, const Date &d2)
{
    return !(d1 < d2);
}

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
bool operator<=(const Date &d1, const Date &d2)
{
    return !(d2 < d1); // (Text has '!(d2 < d1)', but should be '!(d1 > d2)' or '!(d2 < d1)')
}

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
// (== and != are missing in text, adding them based on other operators)
bool operator==(const Date &d1, const Date &d2)
{
    return d1.m_totaldays == d2.m_totaldays;
}

bool operator!=(const Date &d1, const Date &d2)
{
    return !(d1 == d2);
}

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
int operator-(const Date &d1, const Date &d2)
{
    return d1.m_totaldays - d2.m_totaldays;
}

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
Date operator+(const Date &d, int n)
{
    return Date(d) += n;
}

////////////////////////////////Example
Date operator+(int n, const Date &d)
{
    return d + n;
}

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
Date operator-(const Date &d, int n)
{
    return Date(d) -= n;
}

///////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////
int main()
{
    Date today;
    Date next_year_today(today + 365);

    while (today <= next_year_today)
        cout << today++ << endl;

    return 0;
}

docs.title: NAMESPACES
docs.text:
Entities declared globally must have names that are different from each other. Types, objects, functions, and templates declared by the programmer in the global region are global entities. For example, the name of a function and the name of a global object cannot be the same. Moreover, this situation is valid not only for a single source file but also for other source files that make up the project. Using the same name at the global level in the same source file causes an error at compile time. Using the same name at the global level in different files in a way that belongs to external linkage causes an error at link time.

The practical importance of this for the programmer is: If other libraries or modules are used in the project, the names of the global entities in the used modules must not be the same as the global names in the source file the programmer is writing. That is, names at the global level must not collide.

Preventing name collisions is not always easy. Because the modules or libraries used may have been produced by different people or companies. Especially when it comes to large programs, the risk of global name collision is very high. How can name collisions be prevented when libraries developed by different people are combined? This collision problem for global names is known as "global namespace pollution".

Before the standardization period of the C++ language, programmers tried to prevent the pollution of the global namespace by giving very long names to global entities. These names were also often formed with predetermined prefix words. Let's give an example: Let's assume a class that abstracts a dynamic array is defined. If the name of the class is chosen as Array, the probability of name collision increases. This name may have been given to another class within the used modules. A longer name can be given to the class to prevent a possible name collision:

class C_ve_Sistem_Programcilari_Array {
    //...
};

void display(const C_ve_Sistem_Programcilari_Array &);

It cannot be said that this is a good solution. A program written in the C++ language can contain numerous classes, functions, or templates that are visible throughout the entire program. Continuously writing long names is both a troublesome task for the programmer and such names reduce the readability of the program.

"Namespaces" are an important tool of the C++ language that largely solves the problem of global namespace pollution.

Let's imagine our computer's hard disk contains only one root directory. There cannot be two files with the same name in this directory, right? We can create various subdirectories under the root directory. Files with the same names can be present in these subdirectories. For example, a file named necati.txt can be in the root directory as well as in the directories named "notes", "letters", "homeworks" under the root directory.

The global level of the source file, i.e., the global namespace, can be likened to the root directory in the example above. Just as two files cannot have the same name in the root directory, two global entities cannot have the same name in the source file. Just as different named directories are created in the root directory, different name regions can be created in the global area of the source file. In this case, the same names can be used within these different regions.

Namespaces can be defined to hide certain names from the global area in a program:

namespace CDernek {
    class CDString { /* ... */ };
    void push_back(CDString &);
}

CDernek is a namespace declared by the programmer. Every namespace declared by the programmer defines a separate scope. A namespace declared by the programmer can contain other namespace declarations, declarations or definitions of functions, objects, templates, and types.

As in the global namespace, every name in a namespace declared by the programmer must also relate to a single entity. That is, the same name cannot be present more than once in a declared namespace. But since different namespace declarations define different scopes, the same names can be used in different namespaces. Every name used in a namespace is called a "namespace member".

docs.title: Namespace Definition
docs.text:
A namespace definition begins with the namespace keyword. The namespace keyword is followed by the name of the namespace. The namespace name must be chosen in accordance with naming rules. The block following the namespace name contains the declarations and/or definitions of the namespace members. A terminating atom (semicolon) is not placed at the end of a namespace definition:

namespace CDernek {
    class CDString {
        //...
    };
    //...
}

The namespace name must not collide with another name at the global level. The namespace name must also be unique in the namespace where the definition is made. That is, the global namespace pollution problem is not completely eliminated. However, the problem is minimized with the use of namespaces. Making a declaration or definition within a namespace does not change the meaning of the declaration or definition. The only point that distinguishes a declaration or definition made within a namespace from other declarations or definitions is that the declared or defined names are hidden within the namespace. These names can only be accessed from the outside as qualified names, i.e., with the scope resolution operator.

A namespace definition must be made either at the global level or within another namespace definition. A namespace cannot be defined at the local level, i.e., within a function's definition.

docs.title: A Namespace is a Scope
docs.text:
Every namespace declared by the programmer creates a new scope. A namespace can contain other namespace declarations, function declarations and definitions, type declarations, and object declarations and definitions. The same name cannot be used more than once in the same scope. Since a namespace also creates a new scope, the same name cannot be present in a namespace. Since different namespaces specify different scopes, the same name can be used in different namespaces. For example, let's assume a namespace named Project is also defined in addition to the CDernek namespace declared above:

namespace CDernek {
    class CDString {
        //...
    };
    //...
}

namespace Project {
    class CDString {
        //...
    };
}

In both namespaces, the name of the defined class is chosen as CDString. Since the scopes are different, it is valid for the two classes to have the same name.

Let's recall that there are two more scope rules in C++ that are not in C. It was mentioned in previous topics that the first of these scopes is the class scope. The namespace scope is also a new scope added by the C++ language that is not in the C language.

docs.title: The Scope Resolution Operator and Qualified Name
docs.text:
To access the members of a namespace declared by the programmer from outside the namespace, the scope resolution operator is used in the binary infix position. A name written in this form is called a "qualified name". A name declared within a namespace can only be accessed with a qualified name from outside the namespace:

namespace Cdernek {
    class CDString {
        //...
    };
} // (Semicolon was in text, but it's not used here)

void foo(CDString &r);
// Invalid
void func(Cdernek::CDString &r);
// Valid

In the example above, the CDString class defined within the Cdernek namespace is used as a qualified name outside the Cdernek namespace. The parameter variable of the func function declared at the global level is of type Cdernek::CDString &. A name declared within a namespace is hidden within that namespace. To use this name, the compiler must be informed in which namespace to look for the name in question. If this information is not given to the compiler, the compiler first searches for this name in the current scope. If it cannot find it there, it continues the search in the other scopes that enclose this scope. Let's change the example above:

class CDString {
    //...
};

namespace CDernek {
    class CDString {
        //...
    };
}

void foo(CDString &);
void func(CDernek::CDString &);

The parameter of the foo function declared above is of the CDString class type declared in the global namespace, while the parameter of the func function is of the CDString type declared in the CDernek namespace.

There are also tools that allow a name declared in a namespace to be used directly without qualification. These tools are the "using declaration", the "using namespace directive", and "argument-dependent lookup". With the use of one of These tools, a member of a namespace can be accessed directly without the scope resolution operator.

docs.title: The Global Namespace
docs.text:
The region outside all blocks is named the "global namespace" in C++. Names declared in the global namespace can be used directly. In fact, the unary form of the scope resolution operator provides access to a name in the global namespace. Normally, since a name in the global namespace can be accessed directly, it is not mandatory to use this operator. However, in some cases, the name in the global namespace is masked by a name in inner scopes. In this case, to be able to access the name in the global namespace, it is mandatory to use the unary scope resolution operator. Examine the following example:

#include <iostream>

const int max = 1000;
// max in the global namespace

namespace CDernek {
    const int max = 500;
    // max in the Cdernek namespace
}

int main()
{
    int max = 100;
    // max in the block scope

    std::cout << max << std::endl;
    // max in the block scope

    std::cout << ::max << std::endl;
    // max in the global namespace

    std::cout << CDernek::max << std::endl; // max in the Cdernek namespace

    return 0;
}

docs.title: Namespaces Have an Additive Structure
docs.text:
Additions can be made to a previously declared namespace, either in the same source file or in another source file. That is, namespace members do not have to be declared all at once within the namespace. Examine the following example:

namespace CDernek {
    class CDString {
        //...
    };
}

namespace CDernek {
    class CDVector {
        //...
    };
}

The definitions made in the same file above are valid. When the compiler sees a namespace definition, it first checks whether the name following the namespace keyword belongs to a previously defined area. If a namespace with this name has been defined before, it automatically merges the names in the second declaration with the first namespace definition. That is, additions can bemade at another point in the source code to a previously defined namespace. In the example above, both the CDString class and the CDVector class are in the CDernek namespace. There is no situation that causes an error.

The additive structure of namespaces is particularly useful for the programmer when creating libraries. While the declarations related to a module, i.e., the module's interface, are in a namespace defined in a header file, the module's implementation can be added to the same namespace in another source file:

// cdstring.h file
namespace CDernek {
    class CDString {
        //...
    public:
        CDString operator+(const CDString &); // (Text has 'String')
        //...
    };
}

// cdstring.cpp file
#include "cdstring.h" // (Added include)
#include <string.h>

namespace CDernek {
    CDString CDString::operator+(const CDString &)
    {
        // ...
    }
}

By taking advantage of the additive structure of namespaces, the interface of a library presented in a namespace can be divided into more than one header file. The best example of this is the std namespace.

docs.title: The std Namespace
docs.text:
The declarations and definitions of all components of C++'s standard library are made within a namespace named std. The classes, functions, objects, and templates declared in any of the standard header files like <iostream>, <vector>, <string> are declared within the std namespace.

To access a function or class declared in the standard library, the name of the class or function must normally be qualified with the name of the namespace it is in:

std::cout std::string std::cin

To be able to use these names without qualification, a using declaration or using namespace directive can be used, or argument-dependent lookup (Koenig lookup) can be done. It is undefined behavior to make changes within the std namespace declarations, i.e., to make additions to this namespace or to delete some declarations from this namespace.

docs.title: Nested Namespaces
docs.text:
Another namespace can be declared within a namespace. Thus, nested namespaces can be created. Nested namespaces can be used to improve the organization of codes in a library.

namespace CCernek { // (Text has CCernek)
    namespace CStringLib {
        //...
    }
    namespace CMathLib {
        //...
    }
}

The names of the members in a nested namespace are hidden within that namespace. Names in a nested namespace are accessed in the form:

enclosing_namespace_name::nested_namespace_name::namespace_member_name

A nested namespace creates a new scope within another namespace. During the resolution of names by the compiler, nested namespaces are treated like nested blocks. When a name is encountered in a nested namespace, the declaration of this name is first searched for in the nested namespace, then in the enclosing namespaces in order from inside to out. If the name is not declared in the enclosing namespaces either, the name is finally searched for in the global namespace.

docs.title: Definitions of Namespace Members
docs.text:
A namespace member can be defined within the same namespace. The definition can also be made outside the namespace. In this case, the namespace member to be defined must be used with its qualified name during the definition:

namespace CDernek {
    namespace CDStringLib {
        class CDString {
            int size;
        public:
            int get_size() const { return size; }
        };
        CDString operator+(const CDString &, const CDString &);
    }
}

In the example above, another nested namespace named CDStringLib is defined within the namespace named CDernek. The get_size member function of the CDString class defined in the CDStringLib namespace is defined within the same namespace. The operator+ function is declared inside the CDString class. (Translator's Note: The text declares it outside the class, but inside the namespace.) However, the definition of this function is not made within the CDStringLib namespace. Let's assume this function is defined in the global namespace in the following form:

Cdernek::CDStringLib::CDString
Cdernek::CDStringLib::operator+(const Cdernek::CDStringLib::CDString &r1, const Cdernek::CDStringLib::CDString &r2)
{
    using Cdernek::CDStringLib::CDString; // (Added for 'result')
    CDString result;
    //...
    return result;
}

The place where the function's return value type is written is outside the scope defined by the Cdernek::CDStringLib namespace. Therefore, the function's return value type must be written as a qualified name. This is why this type is written in the global namespace as Cdernek::CDStringLib::CDString. In the function definition, the function name is also written as a qualified name: Cdernek::CDStringLib::operator+ (Translator's note: Text has StringLib, assumed typo for CDStringLib)

However, the inside of the function's parameter parenthesis and the main block of the function are in the scope of the Cdernek::CDStringLib namespace. Therefore, the type information of r1 and r2, which are the parameter variables of the function, and the result local variable inside the function can be written directly as CDString. This is because namespace members can be used directly with their unqualified names within the namespace scope.

A namespace member cannot be defined just anywhere outside the namespace. If the definition is to be made outside the namespace, it can only be made in one of the namespaces that enclose the namespace where the declaration was made. In the example above, the definition of the operator+ function must be made either within the Cdernek namespace or within the global namespace. The definition of the function could have been made within the Cdernek namespace in the following form:

namespace Cdernek {
    //...
    namespace CDStringLib {
        class CDString {
            int size;
            //...
        public:
            int get_size() const { return size; }
            //...
        };
        CDString operator+(const CDString &, const CDString &);
    }

    CDStringLib::CDString
    CDStringLib::operator+(const CDStringLib::CDString &r1, const CDStringLib::CDString &r2)
    {
        //...
    }
}

This time, although the function definition is made outside the CDStringLib namespace, it is made inside the Cdernek namespace. Note that the function's return value type and function name are written in the form CDStringLib::CDString and CDStringLib::operator+.

docs.title: using Declaration
docs.text:
It is very troublesome for the programmer to write the qualified name of a namespace member every time to access it. With a using declaration, the namespace member can be accessed directly with its unqualified name. using is a keyword. This keyword is followed by the qualified name of the namespace member. The declaration ends with a semicolon.

using CDernek::CDString;

After the declaration above, the CDString class defined in the CDernek namespace can be used directly in the form CDString , in addition to the form CDernek::CDString . That is, after the using declaration, when the CDString name is used, the compiler treats this name as CDernek::CDString.

The name declared with the using declaration is added to the scope where the declaration is made. The using declaration also carries the common features of other declarations. This declaration also has a scope. A using declaration can be made locally, or it can be made within the global namespace or within another namespace. Like all other declarations, the name added to the scope with a using declaration: 1. Must be unique in the scope to which it is added. 2. Masks the same name in a wider scope. 3. Is masked by the same name in a narrower scope.

Examine the following example carefully:

namespace Mynamespace {
    int a = 1, b = 2, c = 3;
    // other declarations
}

int b = 0;

int main()
{
    using Mynamespace::a;
    a = 10;

    using Mynamespace::b;
    b = 20; // Accesses Mynamespace::b

    int c;
    // using Mynamespace::c;
    // Invalid! 'c' already declared in this scope
}

void func()
{
    int y = a; // Invalid! 'a' is not in scope here
}

After the using Mynamespace::b declaration made in the main function, the b accessed with the assignment b = 20 (text has 10) is the b defined in the Mynamespace namespace. That is, the b name added to the block scope of the main function with the using declaration masks the global variable b. You see that the using Mynamespace::c declaration made after the definition of a local variable named c in the main function causes an error. Remember that the same name cannot be used in the same scope. Before the using declaration, there is already a local variable named c in the block scope. In this case, trying to introduce a second c name into the scope is invalid. Finally, let's take a look at the func function defined after the main function. The value of the a variable is assigned to the y variable, which is a local variable defined in the function. However, an object named a is not visible at this point. Let's remember that the using declaration also has a scope. Since the using Mynamespace::a; declaration was made at the local level, i.e., inside the main function, it can be used without qualification only within the main function. Inside the main block of the func function, the a name must be used as a qualified name, i.e., in the form Mynamespace::a.

However, if the using declaration were made at the global level instead of the local level, this access would also be valid. Examine the code below:

namespace Mynamespace {
    int a = 1, b = 2, c = 3; // (Text has 'mk = c' and 'mi', likely typos)
    // other declarations
}

using Mynamespace::a; // (Text has 'mi', assumed 'a')

int main()
{
    a = 10;
    return 0;
}

void func()
{
    int y = a; // Valid now
}

With the using declaration, it becomes easier to use the namespace member. With a using declaration, only one member of the namespace can be brought into the relevant scope at a time. It can be ensured that more than one name is used without qualification at once with the using namespace directive.

docs.title: using namespace Directive
docs.text:
To be able to access each of the names introduced in a namespace with an unqualified name, a separate using declaration can be made for each name. However, making using declarations one by one for a large number of names is time-consuming. Such declarations also spoil the readability of the program. It has been made possible for all names in a namespace to be usable as unqualified names with a single directive.

A using namespace directive begins with the using and namespace keywords. These words are followed by the name of a previously defined namespace:

using namespace CDernek; using namespace std;

Using a name that does not belong to a previously defined namespace is invalid. All names in the namespace subject to the using namespace directive become usable as unqualified names.

The using namespace directive makes the namespace members visible as if these members were defined at the place where the namespace definition is, but outside the namespace. Examine the following example:

namespace Mynamespace {
    int a = 1, b = 2, c = 3;
    // other declarations
}

void func();
int b = 0;

int main()
{
    using namespace Mynamespace;

    a = 10;
    // b = 20;
    // Invalid (Ambiguous)

    Mynamespace::b = 100;
    ::b = 178;

    int c;
    c = 30;
    // local c
    return 0;
}

void func()
{
    // int y = a; // Invalid (using directive is local to main)
}

The using namespace directive is at the beginning of the main block of the main function. In the source code range where the directive is visible, all names within the Mynamespace namespace become visible as if they were defined outside the namespace, i.e., in the global region. The assignment a = 10; (text has 20) is valid. With this assignment, a value is assigned to the a variable defined in the Mynamespace namespace. The assignment b = 20; causes an ambiguity error at compile time. This is because, at this point, both the global b variable and the b variable inside the Mynamespace namespace are visible, due to the effect of the using namespace directive. The error occurs only if the b object is used as an unqualified name. If the b object is not used at all in the main block of the main function, or if the b object is used with qualification, an ambiguity error does not occur: //... Mynamespace::b = 100; ::b = 178;

The definition of the c variable in the main function is valid. This is because the scopes of the c defined inside the namespace and the c variable defined inside the main block are different. The assignment c = 30 is made to the local c variable. The using namespace directive also has a scope. In the example above, since the directive is given inside the main function, the scope of the directive only covers the main function. It is invalid to use the a name without qualification inside the foo function that follows the main function.

The use of the using namespace directive is extremely easy. With the use of a single directive, all names inside the namespace become directly usable. Although this seems like an easy solution, its unnecessary or excessive use can also lead to other problems. If more than one library is used, when the using namespace directive is used for each namespace to use the names in the libraries in their short forms, the global name pollution problem arises again.

// header1.h
namespace HDR1 {
    void foo();
    //...
}

// header2.h
namespace HDR2 {
    void foo();
    //...
}

// app.cpp
#include "header1.h"
#include "header2.h"

using namespace HDR1;
using namespace HDR2;

int main()
{
    foo(); // ambiguity error
    //...
}

docs.title: Placing a Library into a Namespace
docs.text:
Namespaces are one of the last tools added to the language during the standardization of the C++ language. Since compilers developed before the standards did not support namespaces, many libraries written in the past were not declared in a namespace. The use of libraries not taken into a namespace increases the risk of name collision.

Let's assume it is desired to subsequently place declarations, which were not made in a namespace, into a namespace. For example, let's assume it is desired to add the declarations in the previously written header file named cdate.h to the CDernek namespace:

// cdate.h
namespace CDernek {
    class CDate {
        //...
    };
    // other declarations
}

However, in this case, in a file named app1.cpp, where the codes that use the CDate class are, when the header file cdate.h is included in the source file in its new form with the include preprocessor directive, the names declared in cdate.h can no longer be used directly. Since every declaration made in cdate.h has now become a member of the CDernek namespace, these members can only be used with their qualified names. To avoid making too many changes in the app1.cpp file, a using namespace directive can be placed at the beginning of this file:

// app.cpp
#include "cdate.h"
using namespace CDernek;
//...

docs.title: Namespace Alias
docs.text:
Namespace names must also be unique in the global region. Therefore, namespace names are generally chosen to be long. When using a qualified name, it may not be desired to continuously write the name that qualifies the namespace member. To be able to use shorter names, other -shorter- names that can be used instead of the namespace name can be created. This is provided by the namespace alias declaration.

A namespace alias declaration begins with the namespace keyword. The keyword is followed by the word that will replace the namespace name. Then the assignment operator (=) is placed. The name of a previously defined namespace is written to the right side of the assignment operator. The declaration is completed by the terminating atom (semicolon), as always.

namespace CSD = C_ve_Sistem_Programcilari_Dernegi;

After this declaration, the CSD name can be used in places where the C_ve_Sistem_Programcilari_Dernegi namespace name can be used. A namespace alias can also be used for a nested namespace:

namespace CSDSTR = C_ve_Sistem_Programcilari_Dernegi::CDStringLib;

A namespace can have more than one alias. Namespace aliases are also used in version control in projects. The life of successful software projects continues after the product is released to the market. New versions of some products are continuously produced. When new and old versions of a program are in question, there are code components that both versions use in common. However, some code components are different for each version. Namespace aliases can be used in the creation of dynamic namespaces for different versions:

namespace Ver_1_0 {
    class File {
        //...
    };
    class Usb {
        //...
    };
}

namespace Ver_2_0 {
    class File {
        //...
    };
    class Usb {
        //...
    };
}

int main()
{
    namespace current = Ver_2_0;

    using current::File;
    using current::Usb;

    File f;
    Usb u;
    //...
    return 0;
}

Let's examine the code above: Two separate namespaces named Ver_1_0 and Ver_2_0 are defined for use in separate versions. With the namespace alias declaration made in the main function, it is ensured that the current name replaces the Ver_2_0 namespace. Then, with the using declarations, it is ensured that the File and Usb class names of the current namespace are used without qualification. By replacing the namespace current = Ver_2_0; declaration in the main function with the namespace current = Ver_1_0; declaration, the use of different File and Usb classes can be ensured.

docs.title: Unnamed Namespace
docs.text:
It is assumed that an unnamed namespace exists within each source file. The programmer can make additions to this namespace if they wish:

namespace {
    void func();
    int g;
    class A {
        //...
    };
    //...
}

The names in this namespace can be accessed directly with unqualified names within their own defined source file. In the example above, at a source code point where the namespace declaration is visible:

int main()
{
    A a;
    //..
    return 0;
}

In the main function above, the A name is used unqualified. The A class is defined in the unnamed namespace. It is guaranteed that the unnamed namespace will be unique for each source file. That is, names declared in such a namespace directly have internal linkage.

What is the use of the unnamed namespace? Remember that in the C language, global entities must be declared with the static keyword to have internal linkage. However, in C++, the use of the static keyword for global entities that have internal linkage, i.e., are known only in their own module, has been accepted as "deprecated". The recommended tool is the creation of an unnamed namespace and the declaration of global entities with internal linkage within this namespace.

At the global level, the static keyword can be used for functions and variables. However, definitions of types created by the programmer can also be made within the unnamed namespace.

docs.title: Koenig Lookup (Argument-Dependent Lookup - ADL)
docs.text:
The compiler, encountering a function call, searches for visible functions at the point where the function call is made to understand which function is called.

int main()
{
    func(object);
}

Since a using declaration or using namespace directive is not used in the code above, normally the func function should not be searched for in a namespace. Koenig lookup (from the name Andrew Koenig), added to the language with the standards, changes this rule. If the argument object sent to the function is an object belonging to a class declared in a namespace, the existence of a suitable function named func is also searched for in the namespace that encloses the declaration of this class. This rule is called "Koenig lookup" or "argument-dependent lookup" (ADL).

Compile and run the following code:

#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

namespace NMSPC {
    class A {
        //...
    };
    void foo(A a)
    {
        cout << "NMSPC::foo(A)" << endl;
    }
}

int main()
{
    NMSPC::A den;
    foo(den); // Calls NMSPC::foo due to ADL
    return 0;
}

Koenig lookup is applied automatically. That is, a declaration or directive is not needed to activate Koenig lookup. It is also not possible to disable this feature. The application of Koenig lookup can, in some cases, lead to the occurrence of an ambiguity error:

namespace NMSPC {
    class A {
        //...
    };
    void foo(A);
}

void foo(NMSPC::A);

int main()
{
    NMSPC::A a;
    foo(a);
    // Ambiguity error
    return 0;
}

In the code snippet above, which function is the foo function called inside the main function? The one declared in the NMSPC namespace, or the one declared in the global namespace? If Koenig lookup were not in question, the called function would undoubtedly be the foo function declared in the global namespace. However, since Koenig lookup also makes the foo function inside the NMSPC namespace visible, an ambiguity error occurs at compile time.

docs.title: Friendship Declarations in Namespaces
docs.text:
A friendship declaration is also a prototype declaration for the function given friendship. When friendship is given to a function within a namespace, if the friendship declaration is made within the namespace, the function is also declared in the namespace. Thus, the function given friendship also becomes a member of the namespace.

namespace CDernek {
    class Sample {
        int x;
        friend void foo();
    };
    //...
}

void foo()
{
    CDernek::Sample sam;
    sam.x = 10;
    // Invalid
}

Above, the friend void foo(); declaration made inside the Sample class defined in the Cdernek namespace is also the declaration of this function, so the function given friendship is the void Cdernek::foo() function. If it were desired to give friendship to a function declared in the global namespace within the Cdernek namespace, the friendship declaration should have been made in the form friend void ::foo(); , and the declaration of the global foo function should have been made before the namespace definition.

You see that in the foo function defined in the global namespace, an attempt is made to access the private member of the Sample class. The CDernek::Sample class did not give friendship to the global func (foo) function. For this reason, the sam.x access is not valid.

docs.title: When Should Namespaces Be Used
docs.text: It is clear that the possibility of name collision increases even more when large projects are in question. In a project with hundreds of source files, both the risk of name collision and the additional cost that name collision will cause are high. The use of namespaces is inevitable in large projects. However, the use of namespaces does not provide much benefit in projects that do not have too many files, developed by a single programmer. After all, in case of a name collision, the programmer can easily eliminate the collision situation by changing some names.

docs.title: Cost of Namespaces
docs.text: The use of namespaces does not bring an additional load in terms of the running program's speed or the memory it uses, when it comes to the program's runtime. The controls related to namespaces are done at the program's compile time.

docs.title: C++ Programming Basics

docs.title: STM32CubeIDE Installation & Overview
docs.text:
STM32F407VET6 STM32 Discovery

STM32CubeIDE is the official, fully integrated development environment provided by STMicroelectronics for STM32 microcontrollers.

👉 Download page: https://www.st.com/en/development-tools/stm32cubeide.html

for linux: * Sign up or log in MyST account (required for the download). * install debian linux installer

  • Once the download is complete, you will have a compressed .zip file in your Downloads folder.

  • unzip the bash script 

    unzip st-stm32cubeide_1.19.0_25607_20250703_0907_amd64.deb_bundle.sh.zip

    chmod +x st-stm32cubeide_1.19.0_25607_20250703_0907_amd64.deb_bundle.sh

    sudo ./st-stm32cubeide_1.19.0_25607_20250703_0907_amd64.deb_bundle.sh
after installation complete open IDE and connect to MyST
    follow the licence agreement with space and accept.

create alias 

nano ~/.bashrc
add the end of file 
alias stm32='/opt/st/stm32cubeide_1.19.0/stm32cubeide'
type bash in terminal and try to launch CubeIDE with 
stm32
select a directory as workspace screen will open. launch the IDE

connect to ide your st account from help menu action

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text:
STM32CubeIDE:

  • Is Eclipse-based, so it works on Windows, Linux, and macOS.
  • Provides all the necessary tools for embedded development in a single package:
  • Compiler
  • Linker
  • Debugger
  • Memory analysis tools
  • SWV/ITM debugging
  • Fully integrated with STM32CubeMX → no need to install CubeMX separately.
  • Uses GNU Arm Embedded Toolchain (arm-none-eabi-gcc) for ARM-based microcontrollers.

This makes it easy to develop embedded projects quickly without external dependencies.

docs.title: 2. Built-in Toolchain
docs.text:
STM32CubeIDE includes the following tools:

  • arm-none-eabi-gcc → C/C++ compiler
  • arm-none-eabi-gdb → debugger
  • arm-none-eabi-objdump / objcopy → analysis tools
  • linker (ld) → suitable linker scripts for STM32

Therefore, no additional GCC installation is needed when working inside the IDE.

docs.title: 3. Debugging Features
docs.text: STM32CubeIDE is fully compatible with ST-LINK and supports:

docs.title: SWD (Serial Wire Debug)
docs.text:
• 2-pin debug interface
  • Register, memory, variable inspection

docs.title: SWV (Serial Wire Viewer)
docs.text:
• ITM printf (real-time printing without UART)
  • Live variable watching
  • PC sampling (profiling)
  • Exception analysis (faults)

docs.title: Memory & Core View
docs.text:
• Flash/ SRAM inspection
  • Peripheral register visualizer
  • CPU register view (R0–R15, xPSR, etc.)

docs.title: 4. STM32CubeMX Integration
docs.text:
CubeIDE fully integrates CubeMX, so no separate installation is needed.

  • Clock configuration
  • GPIO, UART, SPI, CAN, I2C settings
  • Middleware selection (FreeRTOS, USB, FATFS)
  • Automatic code generation (HAL/LL)

All can be configured from a single interface.

docs.title: 5. Building Code Outside the IDE
docs.text: CubeIDE contains its own toolchain; however, if you want to build from the terminal, you need the ARM toolchain.

docs.title: Windows
docs.text:
Mingw is for desktop applications, not for STM32 development.

Correct toolchain for STM32: https://developer.arm.com/downloads/-/arm-gnu-toolchain-downloads

docs.title: Linux
docs.text:
If you want to use your own build system (Makefile, CMake, etc.):

sudo apt install build-essential
sudo apt install gcc-arm-none-eabi
sudo apt install gdb-multiarch

docs.title: Hello world
docs.text:
1. start stmCubeIDE 

    stm32

  2. select workspace click launch

  1. Create new STM32 Project

  1. install embedded software packages

select the board

  1. choice the board

  1. project name

  1. gui

in main.c file we will write our first code

/**
 ******************************************************************************
 * @file           : main.c
 * @author         : Auto-generated by STM32CubeIDE
 * @brief          : Main program body
 ******************************************************************************
 */

#include <stdio.h>

int main(void)
{
    printf("Hello world\n");
    /* Loop forever */
    for(;;);
}

but we don't have any display device at our board. how we can see the output of the code.

docs.title: printf ARM CORTEX M3/M4/M7 based MCUs
docs.text:
printf works over SWO(Serial Wire Output) pin of SWD interface.

we can communicate with board from ST link debug circuit(V1 or V2) debug circuit has swo pin and we will debug our codes from there.

lets zoom the arm cortex m4 processor: Instrumentation Trace Macrocell Unit is supported since M3 and above processor. For debug (read memory location, processor related register) SWD(Serial wire debug) SWD has 3 pins and two-wire protocol for accessing the ARM debug interface. one pin use for trace.

docs.title: SWD
docs.text:
Serial Wire Debug is a two wire protocol for accessing the ARM debug interface. It is part of the ARM Debug Interface Specification v5 and is an alternative to JTAG The physical layer of SWD consists of two lines. SWDIO:bidirectional data line and SWCLK: clock driven by the host. By using SWD interface should be able to program MCUs internal flash, you can access memory regions, add breakpoints, stop/run CPU.

The other good thing about SWD is you can use the serial wire viewer for your printf statements for debugging.

docs.title: SWD and JTAG
docs.text:
JTAG was the traditional mechanism for debug connections for ARM7/9 family but with the Cortex-M family, ARM introduced the Serial Wire Debug(SWD) interface. SWD is designed to reduce the pin count required for debug from 4 used bt JTAG (excluding GND) down to 2. In addition, SWD interface provides one more pin called SWO(Serial Wire Output) which is used for Single Wire Viewing(SWV), which is a low cost tracing technology.

the fifo can be called with buffer or register. that fifo is connect to SWO pin. SWO pin is connected to ST link circuit.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: /home : user specific data are stored. /: ROOT. Everything is a file. every linux file and directories are generated from a branch of the root The superuser - also known as root- , has full control over the entire system and this includes unrestricted access to all the files and directories on the computer. /bin: binary, contains essential system programs and commands that are required for basic operation on this system /dev: I/O, hardware, USB, Sensors devices and this is the directory that contains special files which represent hardware components that are connected to your system to your PC. /etc: editable text configuration, configuation files, network settings. /opt: optional, additional software and applications. /usr: User system resources, user-related programs and libraries /var: variable, variable data e.g. , logs, cache or datababes /lib: shared libraries that are needed to run essential system programs. /tmp: Temporary, stores temporary files that are cleared after reboot. accessible to all users. /media....

docs.title: filesystem
docs.text:
• pwd : cprint working directory, start from the root
  • cd : change directory
  • cd .. : go to previous directory
  • ls : list to current directories items
  • ls -l : display with permissions
  • ls -a : show unseen folder starts with dot.
  • ls -la: more detailed show
  • mkdir : make directory
  • mkdir -p new_folder/new_folder : create nested directory
  • touch filename : create file
  • rm filename: delete a file
  • rm -r folder : delete folder

copy a file - touch file1.txt - cp file1.txt file2.txt or - cp file1.txt

copy a folder - mkdir folder1 - cp -r folder1/ folder2 or - cp folder1

sudo dpkg -i <>.deb dpkg -l : list installed packages apt-cache search python3 sudo apt remove : packages and vlc in /etc/apt folder we can see sources.list and sources.list.d

ppa is a custom repository apt-cache search boot-repair sudo add-apt-repository [address] new ppa address added manually in sources.list.d

edit the text file: in linux everything is a file. this is a cornerstone of the UNIX and LINUX design philosophy. for instance info about the CPU can read from /proc/cpuinfo. serial port is communication with other connected hardware with USB devices. and it can be read from /dev/ttyUSB0 etc...

every command line program is automatically connected to three fundamental data channels. Standard data streams abstract way for programs communicte with their environment. stdin : it can be keyboard or other programs output. stdout : it can be terminal or other programs input stderr : diagnostic informations about the program.

vim, nano, gedit.

touch filename.txt

vim filename.txt

to edit a file press enter the insert mode.

to save and exit press first ESC to exit from the insert mode. then type a command starts with a column : and then you can decide what you want to do. for write and quit :wq and press enter.

nano filename.txt for save press ctrl+s or ctrl+o(type filename) for quit ctrl+x

gedit filename.txt graphical text editor openned and not required any command only save and close. if you close it without save, the changes not apply. so type for background process: gedit filename.txt &

echo "Hello from echo" > filename.txt if filename.txt is created before for avoid overwriting use echo "hello from echo" >> filename.txt

cat filename.txt

head filename.txt : show the first 10 line default head -n 5 filename.txt : print first 5 line

files and permissions in many operating systems are files extension like .txt .png after dot is defines the identity of the files.
